title: Green wood drying and seasoning
subtitle: How green-wood projects dry and how to manage it
excerpt: A guide to how green wood dries after carving: the rates, the checking risks, the paper-bag method, and when a piece is ready for oiling or further work. Covers spoons, bowls, stool legs, and basketry stakes separately.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for timber drying rates and checking behaviour. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for moisture-content guidance and the green-to-seasoned schedule. The paper-bag slowing method consistent with Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on treen drying.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor use). Dimensionally stable.

content.text: is not finished when the carving is done. The wood needs to dry. During drying, the piece shrinks slightly, the surface colour darkens, and the wood firms up to its final
hardness. If the piece dries too fast or unevenly, it checks (develops hairline cracks). Managing the drying is part of the craft.

Green wood holds water in two ways: free water in the cell cavities, and bound water in the cell walls. Free water leaves first and quickly, within days on a thin piece. Bound water leaves slowly; this is the process that causes shrinkage and, if too rapid, checks.

Wood shrinks across the grain as it dries (tangentially and radially), not along the grain. A spoon shrinks across its width but not along its length. Uneven walls mean uneven shrinkage, which sets up stress that opens as a check.

Put the freshly carved spoon into a paper bag. Fold the top loosely closed. Put the bag in a cool room away from heat sources. A paper bag slows drying to about one-third the rate of open-air drying. Two to three weeks for a spoon up to 6 mm wall thickness. Thicker bowls and kuksas need three to four weeks in the bag.
Do not use a plastic bag. Plastic traps moisture against the wood and promotes mould. Do not put the bag near a radiator, in direct sun, or on top of a warm appliance.

A rough bowl blank at 20 to 30 mm wall thickness needs four to six weeks in the bag before final hollowing. The bag-drying at the thick stage allows checks to form on the thick walls, where they will be carved away. Final hollowing down to 8 to 10 mm walls happens after the first drying period, then the bowl goes back in the bag for two more weeks before oiling.

Green stool legs and chair components need a minimum of six weeks in a dry space before assembly. The tenon shrinks as it dries. If assembled while still green, the tenon will shrink after assembly and loosen the joint. Dry the legs, then assemble into a dry seat; the dry-tenon-in-dry-seat joint is self-locking.

A spoon is ready when it no longer feels cool to the touch (evaporating moisture makes the surface feel cool). A timber component is ready when a moisture meter reads 10 to 12 percent for indoor use. Without a moisture meter, the old rule is one year per 25 mm of thickness for air-drying larger sections; a spoon or thin bowl is much faster because the walls are thin.

Hairline checks at the rim of a spoon bowl or at the end-grain of a billet are normal. They stop progressing once the piece has reached its equilibrium moisture content. Deep cracks that run through the wall thickness mean the drying was too fast or the walls were uneven. Prevention is the fix; a cracked through-wall on a food-contact piece needs to be set aside.
